title: ISSS608 Visual Analytics
text: Welcome to my coursework website for ISSS608 Visual Analytics & Applications.

title: ISSS608 Visual Analytics
text:
Hands-on Exercises

Hands-on Exercise 1 - ggplot2 Basics
Hands-on Exercise 2 - Beyond ggplot2
Hands-on Exercise 3 - Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
This exercise builds on the foundations of ggplot2 by introducing four powerful extension packages that enhance the quality and communicability of statistical graphics:







Package
Purpose




ggrepel
Automatically repositions overlapping text labels on plots


ggthemes
Provides additional publication-ready themes (e.g. The Economist, WSJ)


hrbrthemes
Provides typography-focused themes optimised for readability


patchwork
Combines multiple ggplot2 plots into a single composite figure



By the end of this exercise, you will be able to produce clean, professional-quality visualisations suitable for academic and industry reporting.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
This exercise builds on the foundations of ggplot2 by introducing four powerful extension packages that enhance the quality and communicability of statistical graphics:







Package
Purpose




ggrepel
Automatically repositions overlapping text labels on plots


ggthemes
Provides additional publication-ready themes (e.g. The Economist, WSJ)


hrbrthemes
Provides typography-focused themes optimised for readability


patchwork
Combines multiple ggplot2 plots into a single composite figure



By the end of this exercise, you will be able to produce clean, professional-quality visualisations suitable for academic and industry reporting.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
2. Getting Started

2.1 Loading Required Libraries


Show code
pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork,
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse)




2.2 Importing the Dataset


Show code
exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
glimpse(exam_data)


Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…


The dataset contains year-end examination grades of Primary 3 students, with four categorical variables (ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE) and three continuous variables (MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE).

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
3. Beyond ggplot2 Annotation: ggrepel
When plotting a scatterplot with many data points, adding labels using geom_label() or geom_text() often results in overlapping text that is difficult to read.

3.1 The Problem: Overlapping Labels


Show code
ggplot(exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_label(aes(label = ID),
             hjust = 0.5,
             vjust = -0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()











3.2 The Solution: ggrepel
ggrepel solves this by automatically adjusting label positions to avoid overlap. Simply replace:

geom_label() → geom_label_repel()
geom_text() → geom_text_repel()



Show code
ggplot(exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID),
                   fontface = "bold",
                   max.overlaps = 20) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
4. Beyond ggplot2 Themes

4.1 Built-in ggplot2 Themes
ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes. The most commonly used are:

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores") +
  theme_gray()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores") +
  theme_classic()







theme_gray() — default







theme_classic()







4.2 ggthemes Package
ggthemes provides themes that replicate the visual style of well-known publications and tools, such as The Economist, FiveThirtyEight, The Wall Street Journal, and Stata.

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores") +
  theme_economist()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores") +
  theme_wsj()







theme_economist()







theme_wsj()







4.3 hrbrthemes Package
hrbrthemes focuses on typographic clarity — it optimises font sizes, label placement, and grid lines for readability in a production workflow.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores") +
  theme_ipsum()









We can further customise the typography using additional arguments:


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
5. Beyond Single Graph: patchwork
In data storytelling, a single chart is rarely sufficient. patchwork allows us to combine multiple ggplot2 plots into one cohesive composite figure using simple, intuitive operators.

5.1 Creating the Individual Plots
We first create three plots separately and store them as objects:


Show code
p1 <- ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of English Scores") +
  theme_minimal()

p3 <- ggplot(exam_data,
             aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "English vs Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()




5.2 Two-Column Layout: +
The + operator places two plots side by side:


Show code
p1 + p2











5.3 Three-Plot Composite Layout
Using a combination of / (stack vertically) and | (place side by side):


Show code
(p1 / p2) | p3











5.4 Adding Tags to Subplots
plot_annotation() automatically labels each subplot, making it easy to reference them in text:


Show code
((p1 / p2) | p3) +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "I")











5.5 Inset Plot
inset_element() allows you to embed a smaller plot inside a larger one, useful for showing a zoomed-in view or a supplementary chart:


Show code
p3 + inset_element(p2,
                   left = 0.02,
                   bottom = 0.7,
                   right = 0.5,
                   top = 1)











5.6 Combining patchwork with ggthemes
The & operator applies a theme to all plots in a patchwork composite simultaneously:


Show code
patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()















Note



Notice the difference between + and & in patchwork:

+ adds elements to the last plot only
& applies the element to all plots in the composite

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
6. Key Takeaways
This exercise let me learn how ggplot2 extensions can significantly elevate the quality of statistical graphics:

ggrepel solves the overlapping label problem automatically — always prefer geom_label_repel() over geom_label() when labelling individual data points
ggthemes provides ready-made publication themes that immediately give your plots a professional appearance
hrbrthemes is particularly useful when typography and readability are a priority
patchwork makes it easy to tell a multi-panel visual story with minimal code — use / to stack, | to place side by side, and & to apply themes globally
Composite figures with plot_annotation(tag_levels = "I") are especially important in academic writing where subplots need to be referenced in text

title: About
text:
About this site

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
This exercise explores the foundational principles of ggplot2, an R package for declaratively creating data-driven graphics based on Leland Wilkinson’s Grammar of Graphics. Unlike Base R graphics which relies on a series of function calls, ggplot2 adopts a structured layered approach that makes visualisations more readable, reproducible, and aesthetically consistent.
The key idea behind ggplot2 can be summarised as:

“The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.” — Hadley Wickham

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
This exercise explores the foundational principles of ggplot2, an R package for declaratively creating data-driven graphics based on Leland Wilkinson’s Grammar of Graphics. Unlike Base R graphics which relies on a series of function calls, ggplot2 adopts a structured layered approach that makes visualisations more readable, reproducible, and aesthetically consistent.
The key idea behind ggplot2 can be summarised as:

“The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.” — Hadley Wickham

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
2. Getting Started

2.1 Loading Required Libraries


Show code
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)




2.2 Importing the Dataset


Show code
exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
glimpse(exam_data)


Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…


The dataset contains year-end examination grades of a cohort of Primary 3 students from a local school. It consists of seven attributes:



Type
Variables




Categorical
ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE


Continuous
MATHS, ENGLISH, SCIENCE

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
3. The Grammar of Graphics
ggplot2 builds every plot from seven layered components. Understanding these components is essential to constructing meaningful visualisations.







Component
Role




Data
The dataset being plotted


Aesthetics
Maps data variables to visual properties (position, colour, size, shape)


Geometrics
The visual marks used to represent data (points, bars, lines)


Facets
Splits data into subplots by a categorical variable


Statistics
Applies statistical transformations (mean, confidence intervals)


Coordinates
Defines the plane on which data is mapped


Themes
Controls non-data elements such as background and fonts



A plot is built by stacking these layers using the + operator.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
4. Base R Graphics vs ggplot2
To appreciate the advantages of ggplot2, it is useful to compare it with Base R graphics using the same dataset.

Show code
# Base R
hist(exam_data$MATHS,
     main = "Distribution of Maths Scores",
     xlab = "Maths Score",
     col = "grey80")
# ggplot2
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "steelblue",
                 color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths Score",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()







Base R: hist()







ggplot2: geom_histogram()





While Base R offers a quick way to produce plots, ggplot2 provides a more systematic and extensible framework, especially when dealing with complex multi-layered visualisations.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
5. Essential Geometric Objects
Geometric objects (geoms) define what type of mark is drawn on the plot. Below are the key geoms introduced in this exercise.

5.1 Bar Chart — geom_bar()
Used to visualise the frequency of a categorical variable.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Number of Students by Race",
       x = "Race", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()











5.2 Dot Plot — geom_dotplot()
Each dot represents one observation. Useful for small datasets to see individual data points.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth = 2.5,
               dotsize = 0.5,
               fill = "steelblue") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Dot Plot of Maths Scores",
       x = "Maths Score") +
  theme_minimal()















Note



The y-axis is suppressed here as it does not carry meaningful information in a dot plot.




5.3 Histogram — geom_histogram()
Used to visualise the distribution of a continuous variable.

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "steelblue",
                 color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Maths Score Distribution",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 color = "grey30") +
  labs(title = "Maths Score Distribution by Gender",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()







Default histogram







Coloured by gender







5.4 Kernel Density Plot — geom_density()
A smoothed version of the histogram, useful for visualising the underlying distribution.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density(linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Kernel Density Estimate of Maths Scores by Gender",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()











5.5 Boxplot — geom_boxplot()
Displays the five-number summary (minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum) and highlights outliers.

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Maths Scores by Gender",
       x = "Gender", y = "Maths Score") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch = TRUE,
               fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Notched Boxplot of Maths Scores by Gender",
       x = "Gender", y = "Maths Score") +
  theme_minimal()







Standard boxplot







Notched boxplot











Tip



If the notches of two boxplots do not overlap, there is strong evidence that their medians differ.




5.6 Violin Plot — geom_violin()
Combines a boxplot with a kernel density estimate, showing both summary statistics and the full distribution shape.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Violin Plot of Maths Scores by Gender",
       x = "Gender", y = "Maths Score") +
  theme_minimal()











5.7 Scatterplot — geom_point()
Used to visualise the relationship between two continuous variables.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Maths vs English Scores",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()











5.8 Combining Geoms
Multiple geoms can be layered on the same plot using +.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(position = "jitter",
             size = 0.5,
             alpha = 0.4,
             colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Boxplot with Jittered Data Points",
       x = "Gender", y = "Maths Score") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
6. Statistical Transformations
Statistical functions (stat_*) allow us to overlay computed summaries on top of raw data.

6.1 Adding Mean to Boxplot

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",
               fun = "mean",
               colour = "red",
               size = 4) +
  labs(title = "stat_summary() method",
       x = "Gender", y = "Maths Score") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat = "summary",
             fun = "mean",
             colour = "red",
             size = 4) +
  labs(title = "geom_point(stat = 'summary') method",
       x = "Gender", y = "Maths Score") +
  theme_minimal()







Using stat_summary()







Using geom_point(stat = ‘summary’)







6.2 Best Fit Curve — geom_smooth()

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "LOESS Smoothing",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Linear Model",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()







LOESS smoothing (default)







Linear model

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
7. Facets
Facets generate small multiples — the same plot repeated across subsets of data. This is particularly useful for comparing distributions across groups.

7.1 facet_wrap()
Wraps panels into a 2D grid, ideal for a single grouping variable.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "steelblue",
                 color = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ CLASS) +
  labs(title = "Maths Score Distribution by Class",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()











7.2 facet_grid()
Forms a matrix of panels defined by two grouping variables.


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "steelblue",
                 color = "white") +
  facet_grid(~ CLASS) +
  labs(title = "Maths Score Distribution by Class (facet_grid)",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
8. Coordinates
Coordinate systems control how data is mapped onto the 2D plane of the plot.

8.1 coord_flip()
Flips the x and y axes — useful for horizontal bar charts with long category labels.

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Default: Vertical",
       x = "Race", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Flipped: Horizontal",
       x = "Race", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()







Vertical bar chart (default)







Horizontal bar chart (coord_flip)







8.2 coord_cartesian() — Fixing Axis Ranges
Setting equal axis ranges ensures scatterplots are not visually misleading.

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Default Axis Range",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Fixed Axis Range (0–100)",
       x = "Maths Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()







Default axis range







Fixed axis range (0–100)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
9. Themes
Themes control the non-data elements of a plot: background colour, grid lines, font sizes, and axis labels.

Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "theme_gray()") +
  theme_gray()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "theme_classic()") +
  theme_classic()
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "theme_minimal()") +
  theme_minimal()







theme_gray() — default







theme_classic()







theme_minimal()











Tip



theme_minimal() is generally preferred in academic and data journalism contexts as it reduces visual clutter and keeps the focus on the data.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
10. Key Takeaways
This exercise demonstrates that ggplot2’s layered grammar provides a principled and flexible framework for building statistical graphics. The key insights are:

Start with data and aesthetics — define what variables map to which visual properties
Choose the right geom — different geoms suit different data types and analytical goals
Layer deliberately — combining geoms (e.g. boxplot + jitter) reveals more information than either alone
Use facets for comparison — small multiples are more effective than overloaded single plots
Apply statistical summaries — overlaying means or trend lines adds analytical value
Fix axis ranges — always set equal axis limits when comparing two variables on the same scale
Choose themes purposefully — theme_minimal() or theme_classic() are generally preferred for clean, publication-ready visuals

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
References

Hadley Wickham (2010). “A layered grammar of graphics.” Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics, 19(1), 3–28.
Leland Wilkinson (1999). Grammar of Graphics. Springer.
Garrett Grolemund & Hadley Wickham (2023). R for Data Science (2e). O’Reilly.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
The core idea of this exercise is to make “static charts come alive”. In the previous two assignments, I learned how to create various statistical plots with ggplot2, but those charts were always “look-once-and-done” , readers could only passively accept the perspective I gave them. This time, I want to turn readers from passive observers into active explorers.
Specifically, I want to achieve three goals through this exercise:

Understand the “grammar” of interactivity: ggiraph abstracts interactive behaviors through three arguments, tooltip / data_id / onclick. This is actually a very elegant design philosophy.
Master two technical paths: ggiraph extends ggplot2, while plotly is an independent interactive engine.
Learn to build Coordinated Multiple Views: This is the most valuable part of interactive visualisation

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
1.1 Loading Required R Packages
The code chunk below uses pacman::p_load() to check, install, and load all required packages in one go. I prefer this over the traditional library() approach because it saves the trouble of writing one line per package.

pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, DT, tidyverse, crosstalk) 

My understanding of each package:

ggiraph: “Translates” ggplot charts into SVG, then injects interactive behaviors
plotly: An independent interactive plotting system based on plotly.js
DT: Renders R data frames as searchable, sortable HTML tables
crosstalk: Allows different htmlwidgets to “talk” to each other (linked filtering)
patchwork: A handy tool for combining multiple ggplots into one figure
tidyverse: The fundamental toolkit

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
1.2 Importing the Data

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

The dataset contains exam results of 322 students, including ID, class, gender, race, and scores in English, Maths, and Science.

glimpse(exam_data)

Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…


My observation: The data is very “clean” with no missing values. CLASS is a categorical variable (string), and the three subject scores are numeric. This structure is well-suited for demonstrating interactive visualisation, there are continuous variables for scatter/histogram plots, and categorical variables for highlighting groups.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.1 Basic Tooltip Usage
The simplest interaction — display data point information when the mouse hovers over it:

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),          # Key: use ID as tooltip content
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618    # Golden ratio — looks more pleasant
)





My thoughts: Notice that we’re using geom_dotplot_interactive() here, not the regular geom_dotplot(). ggiraph provides _interactive versions for almost all common geoms. The benefit of this design is — almost zero learning cost for ggplot users. You only need to change geom_xxx to geom_xxx_interactive.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
section: 2.2 Displaying More Information in the Tooltip
text:
2.2 Displaying More Information in the Tooltip
Showing only the ID is too sparse. In real-world scenarios, we often want to see a “mini profile” of the data point on hover. The trick is to first concatenate a new field in the data:

exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Student ID: ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\nClass: ", exam_data$CLASS,
  "\nMaths Score: ", exam_data$MATHS)) 

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618
)





My thoughts: \n is used for line breaks, meaning the tooltip is essentially treated as plain text. At first I thought I’d need HTML tags, but it turns out a simple newline character is enough. For richer formatting (bold, colours), you need the CSS customisation in the next section.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.3 Customising Tooltip Style
The default tooltip looks rather “plain” black background, white text, no character. With CSS we can give it a complete makeover:

tooltip_css <- "background-color: #fff8e1;
                color: #333;
                font-family: 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif;
                padding: 8px 12px;
                border-radius: 6px;
                border: 1px solid #ffc107;
                box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);"

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618,
  options = list(    
    opts_tooltip(    
      css = tooltip_css))
)                                        





My thoughts: Compared to the “black-and-white bold” style in the original example, I made some personal tweaks, light yellow background, rounded corners, drop shadow. This makes the tooltip feel more like a “modern web app” style. Key takeaway: opts_tooltip() accepts arbitrary CSS, which means with a little front-end knowledge, you can make the tooltip look however you want.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.4 Displaying Statistics in the Tooltip
A more advanced trick, instead of showing raw data, the tooltip displays computed statistics. For example, showing the mean Maths score and 95% confidence interval for each race:

tooltip_stats <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste0("Mean Maths Score: ", mean_val, "\nStandard Error: ±", sem)
}

gg_summary <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                     aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS, 
        tooltip = after_stat(tooltip_stats(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "#5B9BD5",
    width = 0.6
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", 
    width = 0.2, 
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  labs(x = "Race", y = "Maths Score",
       title = "Maths Score Comparison by Race (with Standard Error)") +
  theme_minimal()

girafe(ggobj = gg_summary,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8 * 0.618)





My thoughts: This example made me realise that after_stat() is a really powerful tool, it allows us to reference computed values after ggplot has done the statistical calculation. This solves a core problem: the tooltip shows not raw data but values computed by the chart itself. If you wanted to achieve this manually, you’d have to group_by + summarise first, then plot much more cumbersome.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.5 Hover Effect with data_id
tooltip is “look where you point” information display, while data_id is for linking and highlighting , all elements sharing the same data_id get highlighted together on hover:

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(           
    aes(data_id = CLASS),     # Students from the same class highlight together
    stackgroups = TRUE,               
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618                      
)                                        





My thoughts: This is the essence of data_id, it extends a single data point’s “individual identity” into a “group identity”. When analysing class-level performance, hovering over any student instantly reveals “where the rest of their classmates fall on the score range” far more intuitive than a static plot.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.6 Styling the Hover Effect
The default highlight is orange (fill: orange). Through opts_hover() and opts_hover_inv(), we can fully control both the highlighted and “dimmed” styles:

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #C00000; stroke: black; stroke-width: 1.5px;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity: 0.15;")   # Non-selected elements fade out
  )                                        
)                                        





My thoughts: opts_hover_inv() is what I think is the most clever part, it defines “what non-highlighted elements should look like”. By dropping opacity to 0.15, unselected points almost “disappear”, and the visual impact of the highlighted group is amplified. This is the classic “focus + context” design, emphasising the focal point while preserving context.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.7 Combining Tooltip and Hover
The two interactions can absolutely coexist. A common pairing: data_id for group highlighting, tooltip for showing specific content:

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity: 0.2;") 
  )                                        
)                                        





My thoughts: When tooltip and data_id share the same variable (CLASS), the experience feels most natural , “see all students in this class, and know which class it is”, that’s intuitive double-feedback.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.8 Click-to-Navigate with onclick
onclick turns data points into “hyperlinks” , clicking jumps to a related page:

exam_data$onclick <- sprintf(
  "window.open(\"%s%s\")",
  "https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
  as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6 * 0.618)                                        





My thoughts: onclick accepts arbitrary JavaScript code as a string, so theoretically you can do far more than just “open a new window”, trigger pop-ups, modify other page elements, submit forms, etc. However, there’s a pitfall here: if the string concatenated for the onclick field contains special characters (quotes, backslashes), it can easily cause JS errors. In production, it’s best to do a proper escape.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
2.9 Coordinated Multiple Views
This is one of ggiraph’s most valuable features, two charts linked through a shared data_id:

p1 <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Maths Score Distribution")

p2 <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(title = "English Score Distribution")

girafe(code = print(p1 + p2),    # Use patchwork's + to combine the two plots
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 4,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #C00000;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity: 0.2;")
       )
) 





My core reflection: This is the technique I find most “useful” in this entire assignment.

Looking at a single histogram, I can only know “the distribution of Maths scores”
But with linking added, hovering on the high-Maths region instantly reveals where these students sit on the English chart are they equally strong? Or are they unbalanced?
This kind of “cross-variable exploration” is something static charts simply cannot do

The key to making it work: both charts must use the same data_id (here, ID), otherwise ggiraph doesn’t know how to map between them.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
section: 3.1 Creating a Scatter Plot with plot_ly()
text:
3.1 Creating a Scatter Plot with plot_ly()

plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~MATHS,
        y = ~ENGLISH)





My thoughts: The ~MATHS syntax confused me at first, why the ~? Then I understood: it’s R’s formula notation, telling plotly “this is a column name in the data, not a regular variable”. Once you get used to it, it’s quite concise.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.2 Adding Colour Encoding

plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~MATHS,
        y = ~ENGLISH,
        color = ~RACE,
        colors = "Set1")    # Use ColorBrewer's Set1 palette





My thoughts: There’s a “hidden feature” of plotly here, clicking on legend items toggles the visibility of corresponding categories. This is something static ggplot2 can never do. During data exploration, the ability to “filter out a category with one click and observe what’s left” is incredibly practical.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.3 Converting a ggplot with ggplotly()

p <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = RACE), size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Maths vs English Scores",
       x = "Maths", y = "English") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p)





My thoughts and comparison:

plot_ly(): Independent syntax, requires learning a new system, but offers the best support for plotly’s native features
ggplotly(): Almost zero learning cost (sticks with ggplot syntax), but some complex ggplot features get “lost in translation”

Personally, I lean more towards ggplotly() because I’m already used to ggplot syntax, and in most cases “basic interactivity” is enough; we don’t need plotly’s full set of advanced features.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3.4 Coordinated Multiple Views with plotly
Linking with plotly takes three steps: use highlight_key() to create shared data → plot two charts → combine them with subplot().

shared_data <- highlight_key(exam_data)

p1 <- ggplot(shared_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6, color = "#5B9BD5") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Maths vs English") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(shared_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS, y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6, color = "#ED7D31") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "Maths vs Science") +
  theme_minimal()

subplot(ggplotly(p1), 
        ggplotly(p2), 
        nrows = 1) %>%
  highlight(on = "plotly_selected",     # Triggered by box-select
            off = "plotly_doubleclick") # Cleared by double-click





My thoughts: plotly’s linking has one killer feature beyond ggiraph box select / lasso select. You can “draw a region” and the points inside get highlighted in the other chart. This is especially useful for exploring “doubly-high / doubly-low / unbalanced” students.
highlight_key() is essentially crosstalk::SharedData, it creates a “shared identity” object that lets multiple widgets know “this is the same batch of data”.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
4.1 Interactive Tables with DT
First let’s see what DT produces on its own:

DT::datatable(
  exam_data %>% select(-tooltip, -onclick),  # Drop the helper columns we added earlier
  class = "compact",
  options = list(
    pageLength = 5,
    scrollX = TRUE
  ),
  caption = "Student Exam Results"
)





My thoughts: The search, sort, and pagination features DT provides are completely “out of the box”, backed by the JavaScript DataTables library. Functionality that would normally require lots of front-end code is achieved in R with a single line. This is the magic of the htmlwidgets ecosystem.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
section: 4.2 Linking a Scatter Plot with a Table
text:
4.2 Linking a Scatter Plot with a Table
Finally, a comprehensive example, linking a scatter plot and a table via crosstalk:

shared_exam <- highlight_key(exam_data %>% select(-tooltip, -onclick))

scatter <- ggplot(shared_exam, 
                  aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH, color = RACE)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Box-select students on the scatter plot — the table below highlights in sync")

scatter_plotly <- ggplotly(scatter) %>%
  highlight(on = "plotly_selected", 
            off = "plotly_doubleclick")

table <- DT::datatable(shared_exam,
                       options = list(pageLength = 5))

# Use crosstalk::bscols to stack them vertically
crosstalk::bscols(
  widths = c(12, 12),
  scatter_plotly,
  table
)















My core reflection: This example made me truly understand the ultimate form of “interactive data visualisation”

Charts are no longer the presentation of conclusions, but the entry point of exploration.

The reader can “circle a region of interesting points on the chart → instantly see their detailed information in the table”. This kind of usage transforms the reader’s role from “receiver” into “researcher”.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
5.1 Comparing the Three Approaches
After completing this assignment, I have a much clearer view of these three tools:









Dimension
ggiraph
plotly
crosstalk




Learning curve
Low (extends ggplot)
Medium (plot_ly needs new syntax, ggplotly is easy)
Medium (need to grasp the SharedData concept)


Interaction richness
Medium (hover/click/tooltip)
High (includes zoom, box-select, pan, legend toggle)
Depends on paired widget


Style control
Very strong (any CSS)
Medium (plotly’s own theming)
Depends on paired widget


Linking capability
Multiple charts within one girafe
Multiple charts sharing a highlight_key
Across any htmlwidget


Static export
SVG (vector, high quality)
PNG (requires webshot)
Not applicable

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
section: 5.2 The Core Idea I Took Away
text:
5.2 The Core Idea I Took Away
Interactivity isn’t “decoration” — it’s an “expansion of information density”. A static chart can only present information from one fixed perspective, while an interactive chart lets readers dynamically switch perspectives based on their interests, which essentially turns “one chart” into “infinitely many charts”.
But it’s also important to recognise that more interactivity isn’t always better:

If a chart’s core conclusion is already crystal clear (e.g. “this metric has been declining year-on-year for the past decade”), adding interactivity actually distracts
The scenarios truly suited for interactivity are: multivariate exploration, individual-level lookups, and analyses that need “filter then re-observe”

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
5.3 Pitfalls I Encountered

girafe() measures dimensions in inches, not pixels. I initially set width_svg = 600 and the chart blew up the page.
ggplotly() cannot perfectly convert all ggplot elements. For instance, facet_wrap titles sometimes get distorted. For complex ggplot charts, finalise the static version first, then convert.
crosstalk::bscols requires Bootstrap dependencies, first run may prompt package installation.
When concatenating JS strings for the onclick field, sprintf() is safer than paste0() because it gives more precise control over quote escaping.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
5.4 Directions I Want to Explore
This assignment has sparked a strong interest in interactive visualisation. Next, I want to try:

Combining with Shiny: Embed these charts in a Shiny app so users can dynamically change chart contents through controls (dropdowns, sliders)
More complex crosstalk applications: e.g. a map + a time-series chart + a table, all three fully linked
gganimate: Animate the “time dimension” to create dynamic, evolving visualisations
echarts4r: Another powerful interactive visualisation library, said to perform very well in dashboard scenarios

title: Hands-on Exercise 3: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
5.5 A One-Sentence Summary

This exercise made me realise, ggplot syntax is just the starting point. Making visualisations “explorable” is what truly serves the reader.
